--- Page 1 ---
VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private
Information Retrieval Protocols
Jongmin Kim∗†
Seoul National University
Seoul, South Korea
jongmin.kim@snu.ac.kr
Hyesung Ji∗
Seoul National University
Seoul, South Korea
kevin5188@snu.ac.kr
Jean-Luc Watson
NVIDIA
Santa Clara, CA, USA
jwatson@nvidia.com
Charles Gouert
NVIDIA
Santa Clara, CA, USA
cgouert@nvidia.com
G. Edward Suh
NVIDIA / Cornell University
Westford, MA, USA
esuh@nvidia.com
Jung Ho Ahn
Seoul National University
Seoul, South Korea
gajh@snu.ac.kr
Abstract
While private information retrieval (PIR) enables private database
services by fully concealing access patterns, it simultaneously re-
quires high computational throughput, large memory capacity, and
substantial memory bandwidth. We introduce VIPIR, a versatile
GPU framework that co-designs PIR protocols with GPU acceler-
ation. We develop a unified analytic model showing that state-of-
the-art PIR protocols fall into two categories with complementary
limitations, and propose two protocols that flexibly combine tech-
niques across these categories, overcoming the limitations of both
classes. These protocols incorporate a GPU-friendly data compres-
sion method called expansion-based ring packing (ExpPack), which
offers a high degree of parallelism and minimal communication cost.
VIPIR applies further optimizations to core operations, including
number-theoretic transforms (NTTs) and various matrix-matrix
multiplications (GEMMs). Notably, we develop a tensor-core-based
execution method for database multiplication by interpreting it
as a mixed-integer-type GEMM. We also design memory-efficient
scheduling methods that minimize intermediate buffers and enable
multi-GPU scaling under memory capacity constraints. Overall,
VIPIR achieves orders-of-magnitude higher throughput than prior
PIR systems while reducing communication and memory overheads,
making large-scale PIR practical.
1
Introduction
As data privacy emerges as a key challenge for digital services,
private information retrieval (PIR) [21, 36] has become a critical
cryptographic primitive. PIR enables a client to retrieve a record
from a database (DB) hosted on a remote server without revealing
which record is being accessed. This capability is increasingly rele-
vant in practice: for example, the Ethereum Foundation is exploring
PIR to fully anonymize blockchain access [10], while Apple employs
PIR for privacy-preserving contact discovery and safe browsing [5].
Despite its theoretical appeal, deploying PIR at scale remains
challenging due to a fundamental requirement: to preserve pri-
vacy, the server must perform a linear scan over the entire DB per
query. Single-server PIR [36] protocols execute homomorphic en-
cryption (HE) [1] operations over DB during this scan; HE allows
∗Both authors contributed equally to this research.
†Part of this work was conducted during an internship at NVIDIA.
Hybrid
Spiral
OnionPIRv2
HintlessPIR
YPIR (S)
YPIR (D)
InsPIRe
ExpPack (S)
ExpPack (D)
Double-packing
OnionPIRv2
1
10
100
1000
10000
100000
Server computation time 
(ms)
Server-client communication (KB)
10000
1000
100
DB: 64GiB
Latency bound 
(DB access time) 
Hybrid
Spiral
OnionPIRv2
HintlessPIR
YPIR (S)
YPIR (D)
InsPIRe
ExpPack (S)
ExpPack (D)
Double-packing
OnionPIRv2
1
10
100
1000
10000
100000
Server computation time 
(ms)
Server-client communication (KB)
10000
1000
100
DB: 16GiB
Latency bound 
(DB access time) 
Optimal corner
VIPIR
Prior work
Out-of-memory 
failures
SimplePIR
DoublePIR
(S)
(D)
Figure 1: Server-side online computation time and client-
server communication of prior CPU-based PIR studies and
VIPIR for 16GiB/64GiB DB (single batch). VIPIR supports mul-
tiple protocols (hybrid, double-packing, OnionPIRv2, Exp-
Pack (S/D)). InsPIRe and our hybrid protocol span multiple
DB dimensionalities. Refer to Table 1 for more details.
direct computations on encrypted data, enabling private record re-
trieval without revealing the accessed index. This HE-based linear
scan imposes stringent system demands, requiring high computa-
tional throughput, large memory capacity, and substantial memory
bandwidth simultaneously.
GPUs offer the massive parallelism needed to accelerate such
scans. Compared to server-class CPU systems, which have been
the primary platform for most prior cryptographic PIR studies,
modern GPUs provide significantly higher memory bandwidth via
High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) DRAM and orders-of-magnitude
greater computational throughput at lower per-operation energy
cost.
Yet, only a few GPU-focused studies on PIR exist [22, 35, 56],
and they focus on a single protocol with little regard for adapting
protocol design to efficient GPU execution. Although GPUs have
relatively limited DRAM capacity compared to CPU systems, which
can provision TBs of memory, PIRonGPU [22] and the GPU imple-
mentation of IVE [35] adopt protocols that expands the DB size by
∼4×. This DB bloating becomes a critical bottleneck for scalability.
1
arXiv:2606.11536v1  [cs.CR]  10 Jun 2026


--- Page 2 ---
Jongmin Kim, Hyesung Ji, Jean-Luc Watson, Charles Gouert, G. Edward Suh, and Jung Ho Ahn
For a principled approach, we analyze existing PIR protocols and
develop a unified analytical model that enables reasoning about
them from a common perspective. Despite diverse proposals, we
observe that most PIR protocols fall into two categories under this
model: one (which we term scalar-HE approach) with high client-
server communication overhead and the other (poly-HE approach)
with DB bloating.
In this paper, we present VIPIR, a versatile GPU framework
enabling GPU-aware PIR protocol design and optimized execution
on NVIDIA GPUs. Beyond accelerating existing PIR protocols on
GPUs, we derive new protocols from our unified model that are
inherently more amenable to efficient GPU execution. These pro-
tocols center on an HE primitive called ring packing (or simply
packing) [6, 12, 17, 41] that compresses encrypted data to substan-
tially smaller sizes (0.16% in our settings), trading communication
for added computations. While packing has been used in prior PIR
work [30, 39, 44, 47], we discover its different usage in our proto-
cols and also develop a novel expansion-based packing (ExpPack)
method.
In particular, we introduce (i) a double-packing design applying
ring packing twice to significantly improve compression, and (ii)
a hybrid design repurposing ring packing to transition between
scalar-HE and poly-HE approaches. This avoids the DB bloating
problem of poly-HE, while alleviating the communication overhead
of scalar-HE.
ExpPack augments these protocols by replacing the transfer of
𝑛= 1,280 “special” ciphertexts (120MiB) with a single ciphertext
(96KiB) that is expanded on the server side, leveraging GPUs’ high
computational capability to trade communication for additional
computation. While prior studies also propose communication-
efficient packing methods, they rely on sequential procedures or
memory-intensive precomputation that limit GPU efficiency [39,
44]. In contrast, ExpPack is designed to expose massive data paral-
lelism through thousands of independent sub-operations—namely
number-theoretic transforms (NTTs) and modular matrix-matrix
multiplications (GEMMs)—enabling effective utilization of hun-
dreds of streaming multiprocessors.
VIPIR incorporates various architecture-aware GPU optimiza-
tions to achieve practical performance. Notably, we leverage NVIDIA
tensor cores [20] to accelerate core INT8-INT32 GEMMs by emu-
lating them on the INT8 GEMM datapath, enabled by co-designing
parameters and protocols with the GPU implementation. We also
adjust data layouts for contiguous memory access, apply numerical
techniques such as lazy reduction [19, 33] to reduce instruction
count, and minimize temporary data by carefully scheduling jobs
under constrained GPU memory. VIPIR also supports multi-GPU ex-
ecution by combining DB sharding with batch-wise job distribution,
enabling low-latency scaling to larger databases.
VIPIR delivers substantial performance gains while supporting
DB sizes up to 64GiB on a single GPU (H100 NVL 94GB), while
avoiding the out-of-memory failures common in prior work due
to excessive memory usage. As shown in Figure 1, VIPIR achieves
orders-of-magnitude lower latency than prior work, with our co-
designed double-packing and hybrid protocols simultaneously re-
ducing communication costs. For a 64GiB DB, these protocols attain
execution times of 33.0–33.5ms, only 1.67–1.69× above the 19.7ms
hard latency bound for streaming DB once from DRAM, indicating
that our implementation closely approaches the theoretical perfor-
mance limit. VIPIR also achieves high throughput of 163.1–232.8
queries per second (QPS), outperforming prior GPU-based work,
ShiftPIR [56], by 652.6–931.3×.
We summarize the main contributions of VIPIR as follows:
• We develop a unified analytical model that captures diverse PIR
protocols, exposes their limitations, and enables new double-
packing and hybrid designs.
• We introduce ExpPack, a GPU-friendly expansion-based ring
packing method that exposes massive parallelism while minimiz-
ing client-server communication.
• We build optimized GPU implementations of PIR with tensor-
core acceleration, memory-aware layout and scheduling, numer-
ical optimizations, and multi-GPU scaling.
2
Motivation & Background
Vectors (e.g., v) and matrices (e.g., A) are denoted in boldface; all
vectors are column vectors. An 𝑀× 𝐾by 𝐾× 𝑁matrix-matrix
multiplication is denoted as 𝑀× 𝑁× 𝐾GEMM. Major notations
and symbols are summarized in Appendix A.
2.1
Challenges of private information retrieval
(PIR)
Dozens of PIR protocols [4, 11, 14, 24, 25, 30, 39, 42, 44–47, 49]
exhibit intricate trade-offs across client/server-side computation,
memory access, storage overhead, and client-server communication.
Nevertheless, most constructions build upon prior work, reusing
core components and design principles from earlier protocols.
Without extensive client-side preprocessing [57], PIR protocols
require the server to linearly scan the entire database (DB) per query
to hide the accessed record. The resulting uniform access require-
ments to GBs of data challenge computing systems to deliver high
bandwidth and large capacity simultaneously, while the memory
hierarchy offers limited assistance in alleviating this bottleneck.
In this paper, we distill common factors of PIR to build a princi-
pled PIR framework, rethinking how modern accelerators such as
GPUs reshape the design space. To focus our analysis, we exclude
PIR protocols relying on restrictive assumptions, such as multi-
server non-collusion [21], single-client batch querying [4, 50], or
extensive client-side DB preprocessing [57], which hinder practical
adoption.
2.2
Homomorphic encryption (HE)
HE allows direct computation on encrypted data, which is vital
for implementing PIR. For HE ciphertexts ct1 and ct2 respectively
encrypting plaintexts pt1 and pt2, HE schemes have the following
linear property, allowing inter-ciphertext additions and plaintext-
ciphertext multiplications:
pt0 · ct1 + ct2
decrypt
−−−−−→pt0 · pt1 + pt2
(1)
We classify various HE schemes into two categories:
• Scalar-HE encrypts an integer (plaintext) into a length-(𝑛+ 1)
integer vector (ciphertext).
2

VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols

0
0
0

0
0
0
Database DB
(INT8)
×
𝐷0
𝐷1
𝐷0
𝑛= 1280

(INT32) as SimplePIR input 
Fixed for 
every client
𝐷1
𝑛
B
=
Scalar-HE ciphertexts
(INT32) as SimplePIR output  
Precomputed
Hint
×
⋱

(INT32) after packing
(#𝑅𝑁𝑆× 𝑁)-parallel 𝐷1

(a) Scalar-HE dimension folding (SimplePIR)
(b) Tiptoe-style ring packing
Figure 2: (a) Server-side 𝐵-batch execution of SimplePIR [31] and (b) ring packing based on Tiptoe [30]. Each cell represents an
INT8/INT32 element. Red-bordered cells mark client-exchanged data, where dotted cells are not exchanged under packing.
• Poly-HE encrypts an 𝑁-coefficient integer polynomial (plain-
text) into two or more polynomials in the ring R𝑞= Z𝑞[𝑋]/(𝑋𝑁+
1) (ciphertext).
Specifically, scalar-HE refers to the basic Regev encryption based
on the learning-with-errors (LWE) problem [55]. We use parameter
settings, where we can encrypt an 8-bit integer (INT8) into a 32-
bit integer (INT32) vector. Poly-HE includes various HE schemes
based on ring LWE (RLWE) [43], ring GSW (RGSW) [28], or mod-
ule LWE [38]. For RLWE, which is most commonly used, each
ciphertext comprises two polynomials in R𝑞; i.e., ct ∈R2
𝑞. We use
𝑛= 1,280, 𝑁= 212, 𝑞≃287 throughout this work (detailed in §5).
2.3
Scalar-HE arithmetic vs. poly-HE arithmetic
Computations with scalar-HE are relatively simple without the need
for modular arithmetic [31]. For our typical parameters, plaintext-
ciphertext multiplications and inter-ciphertext additions respec-
tively translate to regular INT8-INT32 scalar-vector multiplications
and INT32 vector additions.
Handling polynomials for poly-HE is computationally more de-
manding. A polynomial in R𝑞has 𝑁coefficients in Z𝑞, where
residue number system (RNS) is used as an optimization to de-
compose the modulus 𝑞into #RNS number of small primes. For
example, we set𝑞= 𝑞0·𝑞1·𝑞2 (#RNS = 3) with𝑞𝑖< 232, converting a
polynomial into a 3×𝑁INT32 matrix, where each row corresponds
to a different 𝑞𝑖:
𝑎0 + 𝑎1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑎𝑁−1𝑋𝑁−1 →©­
«
𝑎0 % 𝑞0
· · ·
𝑎𝑁−1 % 𝑞0
𝑎0 % 𝑞1
· · ·
𝑎𝑁−1 % 𝑞1
𝑎0 % 𝑞2
· · ·
𝑎𝑁−1 % 𝑞2
ª®
¬
While poly-HE inter-ciphertext additions become simple point-wise
modulo-𝑞𝑖matrix additions, plaintext-ciphertext multiplications
involve polynomial multiplications.
The number-theoretic transform (NTT) is a standard technique
for accelerating polynomial multiplications. For the 3 × 𝑁matrix,
NTT applies a variant of the Fourier transform (computational com-
plexity is O(𝑁log 𝑁)) to each row. Once transformed, a polynomial
multiplication (O(𝑁2)) reduces to an element-wise multiplication
modulo 𝑞𝑖(O(𝑁)).
2.4
Ciphertext bloating & plaintext bloating
Each HE category faces a distinct data-size blowup: ciphertext bloat-
ing for scalar-HE and plaintext bloating for poly-HE. Scalar-HE
encryption incurs huge data expansion of (𝑛+ 1) · INT32
INT8 = 5,124×,
which we refer to as ciphertext bloating. In contrast, poly-HE can
exploit the 𝑁coefficients in a plaintext polynomial (∈R𝑝, 𝑝= 218
in this work) to encrypt 𝑁log2 𝑝bits of data per ciphertext. In par-
ticular, RLWE ciphertexts (∈R2
𝑞) incurs only 2 · log2 𝑞
log2 𝑝× expansion,
which is under 10×.
Instead, in poly-HE, plaintexts grow in size as we apply NTT
to enable efficient plaintext-ciphertext multiplications. During this
process, plaintexts in R𝑝are lifted to R𝑞, resulting in a size expan-
sion of log2 𝑞
log2 𝑝×, typically around 4×. This plaintext bloating issue
does not arise in scalar-HE, where plaintexts (scalars) are kept in
their original form.
2.5
Exemplar PIR protocol: SimplePIR
As a concrete example, we look at SimplePIR [31], which organizes
DB as a 𝐷1 × 𝐷0 matrix. To query index 𝑖∗along the 𝐷0 dimension,
the client sends 𝐷0 scalar-HE ciphertexts, where only the 𝑖∗-th
ciphertext encrypts one and the others encrypt zero, forming an
encrypted one-hot representation of 𝑖∗. The ciphertexts together
form a 𝐷0 × (𝑛+ 1) matrix, with which the server multiplies DB,
producing a 𝐷1 × (𝑛+ 1) matrix that is interpreted as 𝐷1 scalar-HE
ciphertexts encrypting the 𝑖∗-th column of DB.
SimplePIR exploits the fact that 𝐷0 ×𝑛input elements are query-
independent and can be reused across clients. Consequently, most
of the DB GEMM can be precomputed, yielding a 𝐷1 × 𝑛hint. If
the client system receives and stores the hint within an offline
communication phase, the client can only send 𝐷0 elements per
query and receive 𝐷1 elements in response during the online phase.
Figure 2(a) illustrates the server-side computations for SimplePIR
with multi-client batching [35]. With hint precomputation, the
server’s online computation only includes a 𝐷1 × 𝐵× 𝐷0 INT8-
INT32 GEMM for a batch of 𝐵queries.
2.6
Ring packing for hintless SimplePIR
Numerous studies [30, 39, 44, 47] have explored hintless variants of
SimplePIR by applying ring packing (or simply packing), which con-
verts 𝑁scalar-HE ciphertexts with 𝑁×(𝑛+1) elements into a single
poly-HE (RLWE) ciphertext with 2× (#RNS · 𝑁) elements. This rep-
resents a compression factor (lower is better) of 2·#RNS
𝑛+1 ×, mitigating
ciphertext bloating in scalar-HE. #RNS can be further reduced to
one after packing (referred to as modulus reduction [9]), achieving
3


--- Page 4 ---
Jongmin Kim, Hyesung Ji, Jean-Luc Watson, Charles Gouert, G. Edward Suh, and Jung Ho Ahn
2
𝑛+1× (<0.16% for our parameters) compression. This eliminates the
need for hint reuse, instead enabling online communication via
compact poly-HE ciphertexts.
Figure 2(b) illustrates an exemplar packing based on Tiptoe [30],
which involves NTTs and parallel GEMMs with additional “special”
poly-HE ciphertexts. Ring packing will be analyzed in detail in §3.5.
2.7
GPU execution model
Modern NVIDIA GPUs are massively parallel systems composed
of multiple streaming multiprocessors (SMs), each integrating exe-
cution units such as INT32 cores for integer arithmetic and tensor
cores for high-throughput low-bit (e.g., INT8) GEMMs. GPU ker-
nels follow the single-instruction, multiple-thread (SIMT) model,
where threads are grouped into warps (groups of 32 threads) and
scheduled to SMs. Performance is governed by occupancy, which is
the number of active warps per SM relative to the hardware limit,
and efficient utilization of the memory hierarchy, spanning per-SM
memory resources (registers, shared memory, and L1 cache) and
global memory resources (L2 cache and DRAM).
Recent GPUs employ High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) to deliver
massive DRAM bandwidth (e.g., 3.94TB/s in H100 NVL 94GB) and
provide high-bandwidth interconnects such as NVLink for multi-
GPU configurations, enabling efficient parallelization with modest
communication overhead.
3
A Unified Approach to PIR Protocols
3.1
PIR as a compression process
We present a distinct perspective on PIR by interpreting it as a
compression process. A trivial PIR protocol has a server return
an entire DB as a response to each query, whereas practical proto-
cols compress the response via HE operations to retain only the
requested records. For example, SimplePIR (§2.5) achieves a com-
pression factor of 4(𝑛+1)
𝐷0
×, compressing DB (𝐷1 × 𝐷0 INT8 values)
into a compact response (𝐷1 × (𝑛+ 1) INT32 values including the
hint).
More generally, PIR protocols apply a sequence of dimension
folding [42, 46] (or simply folding) steps to progressively compress
DB. For a DB with 𝐷𝑘× 𝐷𝑘−1 × · · · × 𝐷0 records, each folding step
removes a 𝐷𝑖factor from the response.
3.2
A unified analytic model for PIR
We observe a dichotomy in PIR protocols, where most PIR protocols
rely on a single HE category for dimension folding. More details
on individual protocols are provided in §7.
• Scalar-HE folding. Building upon SimplePIR, these protocols
have one or two folding steps using one-hot representations (§2.5);
the two-folding variant is referred to as DoublePIR [31]. The second
folding requires reinterpretation beforehand, which treats existing
data as a stream of scalars (plaintexts), because scalar-HE does not
support inter-ciphertext multiplications. Hintless variants add a
ring packing step at the end (§2.6). This PIR category includes Sim-
plePIR/DoublePIR [31], Tiptoe [30], HintlessPIR [39], and YPIR [47].
⑤NTT
Scalar
plaintexts
Polynomial
plaintexts
(ℛ𝑞)
Scalar-HE 
ciphertexts
(integer 
vectors)
Poly-HE 
ciphertexts 
( ℛ𝑞vectors)
⑥Poly-HE dimension folding 
①Scalar-HE dimension folding 
③Ring packing
4 𝑛+1
𝐷𝑖
×
2
𝑛+1 ×
②Reinterpretation
1 ×
3–4 ×
⑦Poly-HE 
dimension 
folding 
2
Di ×
1
𝐷𝑖×
Poly-HE
Scalar-HE
Figure 3: Our unified PIR model. Each arrow, annotated with
its typical compression factor (lower is better), denotes an
operation that directly transforms the response state; the
exact compression factor may vary depending on parameters
and implementation. Here, 𝐷𝑖denote the DB dimensions and
𝑛= 1,280 is the LWE degree.
• Poly-HE folding. An optimized protocol of this category, Onion-
PIRv2 [14], organizes DB as a 2 × 2 × · · · × 2 × 𝐷0 structure, utiliz-
ing RLWE plaintext-ciphertext multiplications for the initial 𝐷0-
dimension folding and RLWE-RGSW external products for the
rest [49]. Although alternative methods, such as RLWE inter-ciphertext
multiplications [4], rotation-based encrypted matrix-vector multi-
plications [42], or MLWE operations [46], are also used, they offer
similar computation-communication trade-offs. This category in-
cludes XPIR [45], MulPIR [3], WhisPIR [25], OnionPIR [49], Onion-
PIRv2 [14], Spiral [46], Respire [11], and KsPIR [42].
We develop a unified analytic model that overcomes the di-
chotomy and provides a comprehensive viewpoint for PIR protocols
(see Figure 3). In this model, the two PIR categories follow these
paths (optional paths enclosed in parentheses):
• Scalar-HE folding: 1○→( 2○→1○) →( 3○).
• Poly-HE folding: 5○→6○→7○→7○→· · · →7○.
There are some exceptions; for instance, SealPIR [4] follows 5○
→6○→4○→5○→6○, although such designs are generally less
efficient compared to other poly-HE folding approaches [49]. Also,
InsPIRe [44] develops protocols that follow similar paths to our
new protocols in §3.4, but InsPIRe exhibits significant limitations
when targeting GPUs (§3.5.3).
3.3
Limitations of the two PIR categories
Under this model, we identify the limitations of each approach.
Scalar-HE folding suffers from large response sizes. For 1○→2○
→1○→3○, the overall compression factor is:
4(𝑛+ 1)
𝐷0
× 1 × 4(𝑛+ 1)
𝐷1
×
2
𝑛+ 1 = 32(𝑛+ 1)
𝐷0𝐷1
.
Although constant terms vary with the parameter choice, the (𝑛+1)
(≥210) term, which stems from ciphertext bloating in scalar-HE,
leads to substantial response expansion relative to the original
record size.
4


--- Page 5 ---
VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols
Encrypt 𝑠0
Encrypt 𝑠𝑛−1
Encrypt 𝑖0
Encrypt 𝑖𝑘
⋯
⋯
Encrypt 𝑠0 + 𝑠1𝑋+ ⋯+ 𝑠𝑛−1𝑋𝑛−1 + 𝑖0𝑋𝑛+ 𝑖1𝑋𝑛+1 + ⋯+ 𝑖𝑘𝑋𝑛+𝑘
Expansion
Used for multiple
Tiptoe-style ring packing
Used for
poly-HE dimension folding
Expansion
keys
Figure 4: A black-box view of expansion-based ring packing
(ExpPack) used for multiple paths in the unified model; red-
bordered boxes denote client-sent data.
In contrast, poly-HE folding achieves a more favorable compres-
sion factor (≃
8
Î
𝑖𝐷𝑖) but suffers from DB bloating, i.e., plaintext
expansion due to the initial NTT ( 5○). The resulting ∼4× increase
in DB size severely limits the applicability and scalability of PIR
protocols for large databases.
The DB bloating problem is particularly critical for GPUs, whose
memory capacity (up to hundreds of GBs per device) is more limited
than that of server-class CPUs (up to TBs). As PIR requires a linear
scan over the entire DB, keeping DB in GPU DRAM is essential
to maximize bandwidth. However, for real-world GB- to TB-scale
databases, the additional ∼4× storage overhead becomes a major
bottleneck, either incurring costly host-GPU communication or
requiring additional GPUs merely for memory capacity.
3.4
GPU-friendly protocol constructions
From our unified model, we derive two PIR protocols that achieve
improved compression for low client-server communication while
avoiding DB bloating, which is critical for GPUs, by starting with
scalar-HE folding ( 1○):
• Double-packing: 1○→3○→4○→1○→3○. Applying packing
twice reduces the overall compression factor to
64
𝐷0𝐷1 , eliminating
the (𝑛+1) factor. This essentially trades off reduced communication
with additional computations for performing packing twice. We
mitigate the computational overhead with a novel ring packing
method (ExpPack, §3.5) that is especially effective for multiple
packing computations.
• Hybrid: 1○→3○→7○→7○→· · · →7○. While prior scalar-
HE folding approaches used packing primarily for compression,
we repurpose it as a bridge to enable hybrid PIR protocols that
transition from scalar-HE folding in the initial dimension to poly-
HE folding in subsequent dimensions. For poly-HE folding ( 7○), we
adopt RGSW-RLWE external products [18], as used in OnionPIR [14,
49].
The overall computational cost of both protocols is dominated
by the first two steps ( 1○→3○); beyond this point, the data is
sufficiently compressed ( 8
𝐷𝑖×) such that subsequent computations
contribute minimally.
3.5
Expansion-based ring packing (ExpPack)
Our protocols are particularly GPU-friendly in part because of a
novel expansion-based packing (ExpPack) method ( 3○) that drasti-
cally reduces client-server communication while exposing massive
GPU parallelism. Across existing packing methods [6, 12, 17, 41], the
core idea is the same: 𝑁scalar-HE ciphertexts are converted into a
single poly-HE (RLWE) ciphertext using additional client-provided
“special” ciphertexts (see Figure 2(b)) and/or keys [12, 39, 44].
ExpPack differs primarily in how the special ciphertexts are
constructed, enabling the server to expand them on-the-fly. Expan-
sion is an HE algorithm that enables extracting RLWE ciphertexts
each encrypting 𝑎𝑖(0 ≤𝑖< 𝑁) from an RLWE encryption of
𝑎0 + 𝑎1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑎𝑁−1𝑋𝑁−1 [4, 49], which we use in a black-box
manner in this work.
We propose coupling this expansion with the packing method
from Tiptoe [30], which is otherwise impractical due to exces-
sive communication overhead for the special ciphertexts. For a
client’s length-𝑛secret s = (𝑠0,𝑠1, · · · ,𝑠𝑛−1), Tiptoe requires 𝑛spe-
cial RLWE ciphertexts for ring packing:
Encrypt(𝑠0), Encrypt(𝑠1), · · · , Encrypt(𝑠𝑛−1).
Their total size reaches 120MiB for our parameters, orders of mag-
nitude larger than the actual index and response ciphertexts (on
the order of KBs), making Tiptoe highly impractical.
Instead, we make the client send a single RLWE ciphertext
(96KiB) that encrypts all the secret values as:
ctExpPack ←Encrypt(𝑠0 + 𝑠1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑠𝑛−1𝑋𝑛−1),
𝑛< 𝑁.
Then, the special ciphertexts can be recovered from ctExpPack through
expansion. More cryptographic details of ExpPack are provided in
Appendix C.
ExpPack thus drastically reduces the communication overhead
for the special ciphertexts, at the cost of additional expansion keys
(up to 5.16 MiB under our parameters). These keys, however, can
be stored at the server and reused across multiple queries from the
same client over time.
3.5.1
Multi-purpose expansion. ExpPack is particularly well-suited
for the protocols in §3.4 as it can be used for multiple paths in
our unified model (see Figure 4). For the double-packing protocol,
the expansion is performed only once, after which the recovered
special ciphertexts can be reused for multiple packing steps. For the
hybrid protocol, we exploit the fact that 𝑛< 𝑁to embed additional
information in ctExpPack: we embed both the 𝑠𝑖values for packing
and the index information for subsequent poly-HE folding steps.
This allows a single expansion process to serve both packing ( 3○)
and poly-HE folding ( 7○) paths.
3.5.2
Massive parallelism. ExpPack unlocks the massive paral-
lelism inherent in Tiptoe-style packing, which was previously pro-
hibitive due to its high communication overhead. After the expan-
sion, whose acceleration via parallelization has been extensively
studied in IVE [35], thousands of independent NTTs and INT32
GEMMs follow (see Figure 2(b)), exposing massive parallelism well-
suited for GPUs.
Revisiting the 𝐵-batch SimplePIR in Figure 2, the 𝐷1 × 𝑛and
𝐷1 × 𝐵SimplePIR outputs are first transformed into polynomials
by treating each length-𝑁vector along the 𝐷1 dimension as a
polynomial in R𝑞, with integer elements interpreted as coefficients.
This yields ⌈𝐷1/𝑁⌉× 𝑛and ⌈𝐷1/𝑁⌉× 𝐵polynomials, to which
NTTs are applied independently.
5


--- Page 6 ---
Jongmin Kim, Hyesung Ji, Jean-Luc Watson, Charles Gouert, G. Edward Suh, and Jung Ho Ahn
Let H and G denote these two polynomial matrices after the
NTTs. The computation of H · C + (0 | G) follows, where C is
an 𝑛× 2𝐵polynomial matrix corresponding to expanded special
ciphertexts from 𝐵clients. As RNS and NTT transform a polyno-
mial multiplication into point-wise multiplications over (#RNS · 𝑁)
coefficients, this reduces to (#RNS · 𝑁)-parallel ⌈𝐷1/𝑁⌉× 2𝐵× 𝑛
INT32 GEMMs with additional modular reduction computations.
3.5.3
Alternative ring packing methods. Unlike prior ring packing
methods, ExpPack avoids both sequential loops that limit paral-
lelism and heavy precomputation that incurs additional memory
overhead. While our unified model does not preclude alternative
packing methods used in studies such as HintlessPIR [39], YPIR [47],
and InsPIRe [44], many exhibit inherent limitations on GPUs. In
particular, HintlessPIR and InsPIRe rely on long O(𝑛) sequential
loops arising from data-dependent, stateful transformations (e.g.,
iterative rotations and accumulations), where each step depends
on the previous, restricting parallelism. They also rely on extensive
precomputation, whose results occupy a large memory footprint; in
our 64GiB DB evaluation, InsPIRe’s CPU implementation exhausted
memory even with 256GiB of DRAM. YPIR avoids such sequential
structures and exhibits computation patterns closer to ExpPack, but
does not provide the same protocol-level advantages as in §3.5.1.
3.6
Putting it all together for our hybrid
protocol
We walk through the hybrid protocol with our optimizations for
a 64GiB DB organized as a 𝑁× 2 × · · · × 2 × 𝐷0 = 𝑁× 𝐷𝑟× 𝐷0 =
212 ×28 ×216 structure of 1B records. In the offline phase, the server
precomputes the hint by multiplying DB with a 𝐷0×𝑛random matrix,
producing a (𝑁× 𝐷𝑟) × 𝑛result. It then performs NTTs along the
𝑁-dimension to obtain H, a 𝐷𝑟× 𝑛matrix of polynomials. Each
polynomial contains 3 × 𝑁INT32 elements (#RNS = 3), occupying
48KiB. Thus, H occupies 15GiB of memory space, becoming the
second largest data object in the protocol after DB. The client also
sends reusable keys (6.09MiB) during the offline phase.
During the online phase, the client sends a length-𝐷0 INT32
vector (256KiB) for scalar-HE folding ( 1○) and one RLWE ciphertext
(96KiB) to be expanded. Then the online server-side computation
is performed as follows:
1○(DB GEMM) INT8-INT32 GEMM between the query vector and
DB (64GiB) produces an 𝑁× 𝐷𝑟INT32 matrix.
3○(ExpPack) First, expansion produces a “special” C (𝑛× 2 poly-
nomials, 120MiB) for ExpPack as well as RGSW ciphertexts
for the remaining poly-HE folding steps. NTT is applied to
the 1○output, producing 𝐷𝑟polynomials (G, 12MiB). Then,
INT32 GEMMs are performed to compute H · C + (0 | G) for
Tiptoe-style packing, yielding 𝐷𝑟output RLWE ciphertexts
(24MiB).
7○(The rest) RGSW-RLWE external products for poly-HE folding
are performed log2 𝐷𝑟= 8 times to extract a single RLWE
ciphertext (96KiB). After 3○, the data size is small enough
that these steps contribute little to the overall cost. The final
modulus reduction [9] yields a 32KiB response (one RLWE
ciphertext with two polynomials and #RNS = 1).
4
VIPIR: A GPU-Optimized PIR Framework
We develop VIPIR, a versatile GPU-optimized PIR framework based
on our unified model. While it supports a range of PIR protocols,
our goal is not to cover all existing variants, but to optimize those
well-suited for GPU execution. By co-designing the protocol with
GPU kernels and execution strategies, we bring PIR performance
to practical levels even for large databases.
The two primary computations in PIR are GEMM and NTT. Two
types of GEMMs appear in the major execution paths of our unified
model:
• INT8-INT32 plain GEMM. This is used for scalar-HE folding
( 1○). As we perform modulo-232 operations as in SimplePIR,
modular reduction effectively becomes free.
• INT32 modular GEMM. This is used for poly-HE folding ( 6○)
and ring packing ( 3○). In this case, we operate on polynomials,
resulting in (#RNS · 𝑁)-parallel GEMMs enabled by RNS decom-
position and NTT. We need to perform modulo-𝑞𝑖operations.
4.1
Scalar-HE folding with tensor-core GEMMs
Since scalar plaintexts fit within INT8, we leverage NVIDIA GPU
tensor cores [20] to exploit their high computational throughput.
For example, for H100 NVL GPUs, which are used in our evalua-
tion, GEMMs with regular INT32 operations are limited to a peak
throughput of 30TOPS, whereas tensor cores enable up to 1670TOPS
for INT8 operations.
As tensor cores support INT8 GEMMs with INT32 accumulation,
we decompose the INT32 matrix into four INT8 matrices. For an
INT8 matrix A and an INT32 matrix B, we decompose B into INT8
matrices B0, B1, B2, B3 to compute A · B as follows:
A · B0 + 28 · (A · B1) + 216 · (A · B2) + 224 · (A · B3) mod 232,
where each A · B𝑖is computed using tensor cores, multiplications
by {28, 216, 224} are implemented via bit shifts, and modulo-232
reduction is free.
We exploit NVIDIA’s prmt instruction [53], originally introduced
for video processing, to efficiently perform the matrix decomposi-
tion. This instruction selects four arbitrary bytes from two 32-bit
registers and assembles them into a single 32-bit destination reg-
ister. Given four contiguous INT32 elements from B, we use two
prmt instructions to extract one byte from each element and pack
them into a single 32-bit register for subsequent tensor-core oper-
ations. This enables the decomposition to be implemented using
only 2 × (# of elements from B) prmt instructions.
All these operations, including prmt-based decomposition, INT8-
INT8 tensor-core computation, and final shift-based accumulation,
are fused into a single GPU kernel to minimize memory overhead.
We further leverage advanced GPU features provided in recent
NVIDIA GPUs, such as asynchronous global-to-shared memory
copies, vectorized load/store instructions, matrix-specific shared-
memory loads (ldmatrix), and swizzling [34].
Our parameter and protocol choices are tailored to enable effi-
cient tensor-core execution. While we have taken INT8 plaintexts
and INT32 ciphertexts for granted thus far, this requires setting the
plaintext modulus to 28 (unlike SimplePIR’s 247–991 range) and
the ciphertext modulus to 232 for scalar-HE. In contrast, packing
methods in YPIR [47] and InsPIRe [44] do not support power-of-two
6


--- Page 7 ---
VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols
ciphertext moduli, requiring modulo-𝑞𝑖(𝑞𝑖< 232) arithmetic as in
poly-HE, which introduces significant overheads in accumulation
and modular reduction under tensor-core execution [27].
4.2
Optimizing parallel modular GEMMs
We optimize parallel INT32 modular GEMMs used in ring packing
and poly-HE folding by (1) reconciling the layout mismatch between
NTT and GEMM, and (2) reducing modular reduction overhead.
In ExpPack, NTTs [35] dominate the computation of expansion
and favor an 𝑁-innermost layout to enable contiguous accesses
within each polynomial. However, this layout is suboptimal for
(#RNS · 𝑁)-parallel GEMMs, where threads operate on matrix tiles
and require contiguous accesses within the tiles for memory coa-
lescing and shared-memory reuse [34]. To bridge this mismatch,
we insert layout transpositions before and after the GEMMs to
convert between polynomial-major (NTT-friendly) and tile-major
(GEMM-friendly) layouts. This enables coalesced global memory
accesses, higher shared-memory reuse, and larger GEMM tile usage,
improving arithmetic intensity and throughput.
We further reduce modular reduction overhead via lazy reduc-
tion [19, 33]. Using RNS primes smaller than 229 and 64-bit accu-
mulators, we defer reduction for up to 32 multiply-accumulates
without overflow, amortizing reduction costs and lowering instruc-
tion overhead.
4.3
Number-theoretic transform (NTT)
We optimize NTT execution by employing a single-kernel design
with Montgomery reduction. For the NTT with 𝑁= 212, whose
working set fits within on-chip resources, we can implement it
with a single kernel, eliminating inter-kernel synchronization over-
head [32, 54]. We also adopt Montgomery reduction [48] instead
of Barrett reduction [7], as it is better suited for the 32-bit integer
datapath of GPUs with fewer instructions [19].
We also fuse kernels to reduce memory traffic between the NTT
and its adjacent kernels. Specifically, we fuse the initial RNS de-
composition with the NTT in ExpPack (§3.5), reducing the DRAM
transfers for temporary results. We also fuse modulus reduction [9]
with inverse NTT.
4.4
Memory-capacity-aware stream-wise
buffering
Although we avoid DB bloating, certain intermediate data objects
occupy large memory space. In particular, the second folding in
our double-packing protocol multiplies a compressed intermediate
(8𝑁× 𝐷1 INT8 per client) with scalar-HE ciphertexts (𝐷1 × (𝑛+ 1)
INT32), producing an 8𝑁×(𝑛+1) result (160MiB) per client, totaling
10GiB for 64 batches; subsequent NTTs for ring packing further
triple this footprint.
To balance batching parallelism and memory efficiency, we in-
troduce stream-wise buffering. For 𝐵-batch execution, we partition
execution across CUDA streams, allocate a fixed per-stream buffer
sized for a single batch, and assign each stream 𝐵/#stream batches.
Each stream processes its batches sequentially, reusing the buffer,
reducing intermediate memory usage from O(𝐵) to O(#stream)
while preserving parallelism. We apply this selectively to memory-
intensive stages.
Sharded
DB
×
𝐷0
𝐷1
2
B (batch)
SimplePIR
input
𝐵/2
B
SimplePIR
output
𝐷1
2
𝐷1
2
𝐷1
2
Batch-
wise 
distrib.
until 
the end
GPU 0
GPU 1
GPU 
0
GPU 0
GPU 1
=
𝐷1
GPU 
1
𝐵/2
Broadcast
All-to-all 
exchanged output
Figure 5: Two-GPU online computation flow for SimplePIR.
4.5
Multi-GPU support
To further scale throughput and support larger DB sizes, we ex-
tend VIPIR with multi-GPU support. We devise two parallelization
strategies for distributing data and computation, both employed
within a single protocol.
First, because DB dominates DRAM usage, we shard it across
GPUs to support DB sizes exceeding a single GPU’s memory capac-
ity. Figure 5 shows an example for SimplePIR, where each GPU
stores a (𝐷1/#GPU) × 𝐷0 partition of DB and independently per-
forms GEMMs with the input queries (a 𝐷0 × 𝐵matrix), which are
broadcast to all GPUs.
After the first folding, we perform inter-GPU communications
to transition to a batch-wise distribution, eliminating the need for
further communications in later stages. If the first folding produces
a (𝐷1/#GPU) × 𝐵output, we perform an all-to-all exchange so
that each GPU holds a 𝐷1 × (𝐵/#GPU) partition of the aggregated
result, effectively reducing the per-GPU batch size to 𝐵/#GPU. As
the SimplePIR hint is shared across all queries and clients, it must
reside on every GPU for further PIR steps. Thus, we instead perform
an all-gather during hint precomputation.
5
Implementation
VIPIR is implemented in C++ and CUDA. We implement the INT8-
INT32 GEMM using the CuTE layout abstractions from CUTLASS [51],
and leverage NVIDIA Collective Communications Library (NCCL) [52]
for efficient inter-GPU communications. We use separate CUDA-
core-based kernels for the INT8-INT32 matrix-vector multiplica-
tions in single-batch execution as they are memory-bound and can-
not exploit tensor-core throughput. VIPIR focuses on accelerating
server-side PIR operations, the primary performance bottleneck;
client-side operations are implemented for evaluation purposes
using SEAL [13], a CPU-based HE library.
We follow standard practices [1, 14, 30, 31, 49] for selecting the
parameters. For scalar-HE, we set the plaintext modulus to 28 and
the ciphertext modulus to 232, which is reduced to 218 after each
scalar-HE folding step. For poly-HE (RLWE and RGSW), we set the
plaintext modulus to 218, and the ciphertext modulus to 𝑞≃287
(#RNS = 3), which can be reduced to 𝑞≃229 (#RNS = 1) through
modulus reduction [9], where slightly larger parameters are used
for implementing OnionPIRv2 [14]. We verify that these parameters
achieve 128-bit security when using degrees of 𝑛= 1,280 and
𝑁= 212 based on Lattice Estimator [2]. Parameter configurations
are more detailed in Appendix A and Appendix D.
7


--- Page 8 ---
Jongmin Kim, Hyesung Ji, Jean-Luc Watson, Charles Gouert, G. Edward Suh, and Jung Ho Ahn
Table 1: Performance comparison of hintless PIR implementations across various DB sizes. The first three use only poly-HE
folding; the next six use scalar-HE folding based on SimplePIR (S) or DoublePIR (D) [31]; and the last two combine both
poly-HE and scalar-HE folding. Green cells denote the best value in each group. We report single-batch (𝐵=1) server-side online
execution times in milliseconds. Communication per query (Comm./query) includes non-reusable keys, but excludes reusable
keys (Reuse-keys). We used one H100 NVL 94GB GPU for VIPIR. OoM refers to out-of-memory failures.

6
Evaluation
6.1
Experimental setup
We tested VIPIR across multiple PIR protocols, including exist-
ing ones—OnionPIRv2 [14] and SimplePIR/DoublePIR [31]; hint-
less protocols differing from prior work [30, 39, 47] only in ring
packing—ExpPack(S) (SimplePIR-based: 1○→3○) and ExpPack(D)
(DoublePIR-based: 1○→2○→1○→3○); plus our double-packing
and hybrid protocols.
We primarily evaluated the performance of VIPIR on an H100
NVL 94GB GPU with an effective HBM bandwidth of 3.48TB/s. To
demonstrate multi-GPU scalability, we also conducted experiments
using two H100 NVL 94GB GPUs connected via NVLink 3.0, of-
fering a bandwidth of 530.16GB/s. All reported execution times
reflect server-side online execution times only, with throughput
measured in queries per second (QPS). When DB contains 𝐷1B
records, we set 𝐷0 ≈
√
𝐷for SimplePIR and DoublePIR (both with
and without ExpPack). For double-packing and hybrid protocols,
we used 𝐷/𝐷0 = 220 to minimize communication, except for 64GiB
DB, where we used 𝐷/𝐷0 = 219 by default to avoid out-of-memory
failures under large-batch execution (Table 1 still uses 220 as it
reports single-batch results).
We compare VIPIR against state-of-the-art PIR studies, includ-
ing Spiral [46], OnionPIRv2 [14], HintlessPIR [39], YPIR [47], In-
sPIRe [44], and SimplePIR/DoublePIR [31]. Their open-source CPU
versions were executed on a system with an Intel Xeon Gold 6348
CPU and 256GiB of DRAM. Configurations for the baselines are
described in Appendix B.
6.2
VIPIR vs. CPU baselines
Our extensive comparison against prior CPU baselines (Table 1)
shows that VIPIR achieves orders-of-magnitude higher performance
in server-side PIR computations than all the baselines. Compared
to DoublePIR-based YPIR [47], which shows the best performance
among the baselines, even our slowest configuration, ExpPack(D)
demonstrates 12.5–77.9× improvements. Notably, the performance
gains increase with the DB size—12.5× for 1GiB and 77.9× for 64GiB—
highlighting VIPIR’s strong scalability to large databases.
Owing to our memory-capacity-aware design, which introduces
minimal overhead for auxiliary data objects, VIPIR can efficiently
accommodate large databases within limited memory budgets. In
contrast, InsPIRe [44] and poly-HE folding approaches, such as
Spiral [46] and OnionPIRv2 [14], encounter out-of-memory (OoM)
failures even with 256GiB of host memory. VIPIR, however, suc-
cessfully handles up to 64GiB of DB entirely on the GPU, despite
having only 94GiB of available memory.
Our new protocol constructions demonstrate promising reduc-
tions in communication cost while maintaining competitive server-
side computation performance, even with their additional computa-
tion overhead. The double-packing and hybrid protocols achieve the
lowest per-query communication cost within their respective proto-
col groups. At the same time, their server-side performance matches
8


--- Page 9 ---
VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols

based on the CPU implementation of YPIR [47], which does
not support batching, and 32-batched VIPIR.

that of simple designs such as ExpPack(S) for large databases.
For 64GiB DB, ExpPack(S) requires 28.3ms of server computation,
whereas the double-packing and hybrid protocols require compara-
ble 33.0ms and 33.5ms, respectively.
64GiB PIR execution times of 33.0–33.5ms with our protocols
are only 1.67–1.69× above the hard latency bound imposed by DB
access time. Simply streaming 64GiB of data from DRAM requires
19.7ms on our evaluated GPU system.
VIPIR also excels in executing existing protocols. Compared
to the CPU implementation of OnionPIRv2 [14], VIPIR achieves
283–422× performance improvements with the identical protocol.
Although VIPIR currently incurs twice the communication cost of
the CPU baseline, this can be equalized by replacing half of the
transmitted data with random seeds [3], as done in the baseline.
Support for multi-client batching [35] can further widen the
performance gap when evaluating throughput (QPS). While prior
CPU-based studies provide only limited batching capabilities, VIPIR
fully supports batching as long as memory space is sufficient. As
shown in Table 2, under identical SimplePIR/DoublePIR protocols,
32-batch execution with VIPIR achieves 188–1,029× higher QPS
compared to the single-batch CPU implementation [47].
6.3
VIPIR vs. GPU baselines
Several GPU-based PIR studies also exist. We compare VIPIR against
PIRonGPU [22], which was executed on the same GPU system,
as well as the reported performance of ShiftPIR [56]. PIRonGPU
and ShiftPIR belong to poly-HE folding approaches, which incur
significant DB bloating due to NTT, limiting scalability to large DB
sizes. ShiftPIR [56] attempts to mitigate this by partitioning DB and
overlapping host-to-device transfers with GPU computation, but
becomes constrained by the low host-device bandwidth.
As shown in Table 3, VIPIR consistently outperforms prior GPU-
based PIR implementations across all DB sizes and all protocol con-
figurations. Even when using OnionPIRv2, which also incurs DB
bloating, VIPIR achieves 65.1–75.0× and 41.3–49.6× QPS improve-
ments compared to PIRonGPU and ShiftPIR, respectively. Further-
more, using our protocols without DB bloating (all protocols in
Table 3 except OnionPIRv2) yields larger QPS gains, reaching 175.7–
405.1× for 16GiB DB and 652.6–931.3× for 64GiB.
6.4
Sensitivity study: Execution parameters
Figure 6 shows how throughput (QPS) varies with DB size, batch size,
and protocol. For all protocols, QPS increases steadily as the batch
size grows. Relative to single-batch execution, 32-batch execution
improves GPU throughput by 3.69–6.62×, 11.33–15.06×, 2.64–4.95×,
and 3.33–6.02× for ExpPack(S), ExpPack(D), double-packing, and
hybrid protocols, respectively. The particularly strong scaling of
ExpPack(D) stems from its minimal reliance on ring packing, which
is applied only to O(𝑛2) data elements and is thus independent
of the DB dimensions. This implies that our tensor-core GEMM
implementation for dimension folding scales well with batching.
The QPS gap among the protocols narrows as the DB size in-
creases, which makes our double-packing and hybrid protocols
more attractive for large databases. Using 32-batch execution as
the reference point, for a 16GiB DB, ExpPack(S) achieves 2.31× and
1.81× higher QPS than the double-packing and hybrid protocols,
respectively. When the DB size increases to 64GiB, however, these
gaps shrink to 1.43× and 1.18×. This is because the overall execution
time becomes increasingly dominated by the DB GEMM performed
in the first scalar-HE folding step, while steps such as expansion
remain a constant overhead.
Figure 6 also shows the peak device memory usage for each
case. For OnionPIR, even a single-batch execution with a 16GiB DB
already uses 66.9GiB of memory because of the 4× DB bloating. The
other protocols based on scalar-HE folding do not suffer from this
issue; even for a 64GiB DB and a batch size of up to 32, they use
only 69.8–92.1GiB of memory.
6.5
Sensitivity study: GPU optimizations
Figure 7 shows the 32-batch execution time breakdown as we incre-
mentally apply our GPU optimizations targeting GEMMs. For the
64GiB DB, without tensor cores (Base), the initial DB GEMM uses
standard INT32 operations and accounts for 60.5–74.0% of total
server-side online computation time. Our tensor-core DB GEMM
implementation accelerates this by 3.89–4.05×, reducing its share
to 29.8–42.2%. Applying transposed INT32 GEMMs further acceler-
ates the GEMMs in the first ExpPack computation by 1.09–1.44×.
Together, these two GEMM optimizations deliver 1.44–1.53× end-
to-end speedups for the 16GiB DB, and much larger 2.04–2.29×
speedups for the 64GiB DB.
Additionally, thanks to our efficient multi-GPU partitioning
strategies, VIPIR achieves near-perfect scaling with 1.94–2.01×
speedups when using two GPUs, where speedups also come from
reduced per-GPU working set. While VIPIR supports more GPUs,
we leave their evaluation as future work.
9

Figure 7: Online server computation latency as our GPU
optimizations—tensor-core INT8-INT32 GEMMs (+Tensor),
transposed INT32 GEMMs (+Transpose), and two-GPU execu-
tion (+2-GPU)—are incrementally applied. We utilized H100
NVL 94GB GPUs with NVLink connections.
6.6
Deeper analysis of the hybrid protocol
Determining the DB dimensionality is a critical design choice for
protocol constructions, with particularly substantial impacts on the
hybrid protocol. Suppose DB contains 𝐷records in total. Figure 8
shows that varying 𝐷0 creates a trade-off between server compu-
tation and client-server communication. Increasing 𝐷0 (decreas-
ing 𝐷/𝐷0) yields more compressed outputs (compression factor:
4(𝑛+1)
𝐷0
×) from the initial folding, reducing the data volume for ring
packing. In contrast, 𝐷0 has negligible impact on DB GEMM time,
as the (𝐷/𝐷0) × 𝐵× 𝐷0 GEMM involves the same 𝐷· 𝐵multiply-
accumulates regardless of 𝐷0, for batch size 𝐵. Therefore, increasing
𝐷0 reduces overall server execution time but increases communica-
tion volume (O(𝐷0)) to send the encrypted one-hot representation
of the index in the 𝐷0 dimension.
We primarily used 𝐷/𝐷0 = 220 in our experiments to min-
imize server-client communication, as computational costs are
largely mitigated by our optimizations. If we instead opt for more
communication-heavy settings with 𝐷/𝐷0 = 217, we can obtain
1.59–1.76× speedups (vs. 220) for DB sizes smaller than 64GiB, and
1.21× for 64GiB (vs. 219). We leverage this trade-off to show multiple
data points in Figure 1.
7
Related Work
Since the seminal work of Chor et al. [21], numerous PIR protocols
have been proposed. XPIR [45] employs a single poly-HE folding
0
400
800
1200
1600
2000
2400
0
30
60
90
120
150
180
256 128 64
32 256 128 64
32 256 128 64
32 256 128 64
32
8GiB
16GiB
32GiB
64GiB
Online communication (KB)
Latency (ms)
DB GEMM
1st ExpPack (Exp)
1st ExpPack (GEMM)
Etc.
Comm./query
𝑫/𝑫𝟎220 219 218 217 220 219 218 217 220 219 218 217 220 219 218 217
OoM
Batch=32, Hybrid
Figure 8: Latency breakdown and online communication as
𝐷/𝐷0 varies in the hybrid protocol on an H100 NVL 94GB
GPU. OoM refers to out-of-memory failures.
step. SealPIR [4] introduces query expansion and performs mul-
tiple poly-HE folding steps with a reinterpretation step [8, 26].
WhisPIR [25] later introduces an expansion method with reduced
expansion key overhead. Building on SealPIR, MulPIR [3] employs
RLWE inter-ciphertext multiplications, while OnionPIR [49] further
improves efficiency by using RGSW-RLWE external products [18]
for non-initial folding steps. OnionPIRv2 [14] further optimizes
the design with hardware-friendly implementations and protocol
fine-tuning. Spiral [46] explores MLWE [38] in place of RLWE, and
Respire [11] leverages subring-based RLWE dimension reduction
for small-record databases. KsPIR [42] instead proposes using en-
crypted RLWE matrix-vector multiplications for poly-HE folding.
In parallel, scalar-HE dimension folding has gained traction since
its introduction in SimplePIR [31]. SimplePIR reduces communica-
tion by requiring clients to download a large reusable hint. How-
ever, this hint must be replaced for every DB update and requires a
large client-side storage. Hintless protocols, including Tiptoe [30],
HintlessPIR [39], and YPIR [47], apply various ring packing meth-
ods to SimplePIR, building on packing techniques used in other
contexts [12, 17, 41]. There are potentially more packing methods
that may be applicable to PIR, such as HERMES [6]. InsPIRe [44]
proposes a new packing method that leverages heavy precomputa-
tion to reduce online computation cost, and introduces a poly-HE
folding technique that interpolates DB entries into a function.
Despite extensive work on improving PIR protocols, relatively
few studies focus on their hardware acceleration. PIRonGPU [22]
provides an open-source GPU implementation of SealPIR [4] using
the HEonGPU library [23]. ShiftPIR [56] proposes a GPU-aware
protocol that reduces client overhead by partially offloading query
10


--- Page 11 ---
VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols
generation to the server. Lam et al. [37] and CIP-PIR [29] explore
GPU acceleration for multi-server PIR protocols, which rely on
non-collusion assumptions that we consider restrictive and thus do
not address in this work.
Several recent architecture proposals also target PIR accelera-
tion. IVE [35] proposes an ASIC accelerator design dedicated for
processing batches of OnionPIR queries. INSPIRE [40] (distinct
from InsPIRe) introduces an in-storage accelerator for PIR on SSDs.
SmartPIR [16] extends this approach by offloading HE operations
to computational storage devices (CSDs). Conflux [15] similarly
leverages CSDs for keyword PIR, an extension of index-based PIR.
8
Conclusion
We presented VIPIR, a versatile GPU framework that rethinks the
design and implementation of PIR through a unified, GPU-centric
perspective. By introducing a unified analytical model, we expose
fundamental trade-offs in prior PIR protocols, particularly the ten-
sion between ciphertext and DB (plaintext) bloating. Guided by these
insights, we propose double-packing and hybrid protocols that
improve communication efficiency without incurring prohibitive
memory capacity demands. A key enabler is ExpPack, a novel ring
packing technique that reduces communication cost while expos-
ing massive parallelism well-suited for GPUs. VIPIR also includes
various architecture-aware optimizations, including tensor-core-
accelerated GEMMs, optimized NTT execution, memory-efficient
scheduling, and multi-GPU support. Extensive evaluation shows
that VIPIR achieves orders-of-magnitude performance improve-
ments over prior CPU- and GPU-based PIR systems, while also
reducing communication costs and avoiding out-of-memory fail-
ures.

morphic encryption. The Journal of Supercomputing 78, 2 (2022), 2840–2872.
doi:10.1007/s11227-021-03980-5
[55] Oded Regev. 2009. On Lattices, Learning with Errors, Random Linear Codes, and
Cryptography. J. ACM 56, 6, Article 34 (2009), 40 pages. doi:10.1145/1568318.
1568324
[56] Zihan Wang, Lutan Zhao, Ming Luo, Zhiwei Wang, Haoqi He, Wenzhe Lv, Xuan
Ding, Dan Meng, and Rui Hou. 2025. ShiftPIR: An Efficient PIR System with
Gravity Shifting from Client to Server. In ACM Conference on Computer and
Communications Security. 1143–1157. doi:10.1145/3719027.3765153
[57] Mingxun Zhou, Andrew Park, Wenting Zheng, and Elaine Shi. 2024. Piano:
Extremely Simple, Single-Server PIR with Sublinear Server Computation. In IEEE
Symposium on Security and Privacy (SP). doi:10.1109/SP54263.2024.00055
A
Cryptographic Background & Symbols
We introduce the cryptographic constructions of LWE-based scalar-
HE and RLWE-based poly-HE in this appendix.
A.1
LWE scalar-HE and its parameters
LWE has a plaintext modulus 𝑝LWE and a ciphertext modulus 𝑞LWE.
For an LWE degree 𝑛, the client samples a length-𝑛secret vector
s = (𝑠0,𝑠1, · · · ,𝑠𝑛−1) ∈{−1, 0, 1}𝑛from a uniform ternary distri-
bution, a length-𝑛random vector a = (𝑎0,𝑎1, · · · ,𝑎𝑛−1) ∈Z𝑛
𝑞LWE
from a uniform distribution in Z𝑞LWE, and an error value 𝑒∈Z
from a discrete Gaussian distribution with a standard deviation 𝜎.
Typically, 𝜎is set to 3.2 and 𝑒is clipped to ensure that |𝑒| < 6𝜎.
We can encrypt a single plaintext scalar 𝑚∈Z𝑝LWE by the fol-
lowing:
Encrypt(𝑚) →ct = (a,𝑏) ∈Z𝑛+1
𝑞LWE,
𝑏+ a𝑇s = 𝑏+
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝑎𝑖· 𝑠𝑖= Δ · 𝑚+ 𝑒
(mod 𝑞LWE)
Here, Δ is called the scale and is determined as Δ = 𝑞LWE
𝑝LWE .
It is convenient to define the phase of an LWE ciphertext:
𝜙(ct) = 𝑏+ a𝑇s = Δ · 𝑚+ 𝑒
(mod 𝑞LWE),
𝜙(ct) ∈
h
−𝑞LWE
2
, 𝑞LWE
2
−1
i
The client can recover the plaintext 𝑚∈[−𝑝LWE
2 , 𝑝LWE
2
] by com-
puting
Decrypt(ct) = round
 1
Δ · 𝜙(ct)

= round(𝑚+ 𝑒
Δ) →𝑚,
provided that |𝑒| < Δ
2 .
For ciphertexts ct = (a,𝑏) and ct′ = (a′,𝑏′), we can perform
following inter-ciphertext additions and plaintext-ciphertext multi-
plications:
ct + ct′ = (a + a′,𝑏+ 𝑏′) ∈Z𝑛+1
𝑞LWE
𝑐· ct𝑖= (𝑐· a,𝑐· 𝑏) ∈Z𝑛+1
𝑞LWE
The phase of the result will be (for sufficiently small 𝑐and 𝑚):
𝜙(ct + ct′) = Δ · (𝑚+ 𝑚′) + (𝑒+ 𝑒′) = 𝜙(ct) + 𝜙(ct′)
𝜙(𝑐· ct) = Δ · (𝑐· 𝑚) + (𝑐· 𝑒) = 𝑐· 𝜙(ct)
In this paper, we exclusively use the following parameters for
LWE: 𝑝LWE = 28, 𝑞LWE = 232, 𝜎= 3.2, 𝑛= 1,280, and Δ = 224.
The notations 𝑝LWE and 𝑞LWE are only used in this subsection of
appendix.
A.2
RLWE poly-HE and its parameters
RLWE also has a plaintext modulus 𝑝and a ciphertext modulus
𝑞. Let R𝑞= Z𝑞[𝑋]/(𝑋𝑁+ 1) be the cyclotomic polynomial ring
with a power-of-two polynomial ring degree 𝑁. The client samples
a secret polynomial 𝑠(𝑋) = 𝑠0 + 𝑠1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑠𝑁−1𝑋𝑁−1, where
each 𝑠𝑖is sampled from a uniform ternary distribution in {−1, 0, 1}.
RLWE enables encrypting a plaintext polynomial 𝑚(𝑋) = 𝑚0 +
𝑚1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑚𝑁−1𝑋𝑁−1 ∈R𝑝in a ciphertext. Thus, we can put
𝑁log𝑝bits of information in a single ciphertext. For encrypting
𝑚(𝑋), the client samples a random polynomial 𝑎(𝑋) ∈R𝑞, whose
coefficients are sampled from a uniform distribution in Z𝑞, and
an error polynomial 𝑒(𝑋) ∈Z[𝑋]/(𝑋𝑁+ 1), whose coefficients
12


--- Page 13 ---
VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols
are sampled from a discrete Gaussian distribution with a standard
deviation 𝜎. Then, the client computes the following:
Encrypt(𝑚(𝑋)) →ct = (𝑎(𝑋),𝑏(𝑋)) ∈R2
𝑞
𝑎(𝑋) · 𝑠(𝑋) + 𝑏(𝑋) = Δ · 𝑚(𝑋) + 𝑒(𝑋)
(mod 𝑞)
Here, Δ is determined as Δ = ⌊𝑞
𝑝⌋(floored result).
We define the phase of an RLWE ciphertext in the similar way
as LWE:
𝜙(ct) = 𝑎(𝑋) · 𝑠(𝑋) + 𝑏(𝑋) = Δ · 𝑚(𝑋) + 𝑒(𝑋)
(mod 𝑞),
where each coefficient of the phase is in
h
−𝑞−1
2 , 𝑞−1
2
i
range.
The client can recover the plaintext 𝑚(𝑋) with its coefficients
in
h
−𝑝−1
2 , 𝑝−1
2
i
range by computing
Decrypt(ct) = round
 1
Δ · 𝜙(ct)

,
given that each coefficient of 𝑒(𝑋) is in ␀−Δ
2 , Δ
2
 range.
Inter-ciphertext additions and plaintext-ciphertext multiplica-
tions are performed by polynomial additions and polynomial multi-
plications, respectively. For ct = (𝑎(𝑋),𝑏(𝑋)) and ct′ = (𝑎′(𝑋),𝑏′(𝑋)),
they are computed as follows:
ct + ct′ = (𝑎(𝑋) + 𝑎′(𝑋),𝑏(𝑋) + 𝑏′(𝑋))
𝑐(𝑋) · ct = (𝑐(𝑋) · 𝑎(𝑋),𝑐(𝑋) · 𝑏(𝑋))
In this paper, we use 𝑝= 218, 𝑞≃287 (#RNS = 3), 𝜎= 3.2,
𝑁= 212, and Δ = ⌊𝑞
𝑝⌋≃269 as the default parameters. However,
for our implementation of the OnionPIRv2 [14] protocol, we use
𝑝= 232, 𝑞≃2108 (#RNS = 4), and Δ ≃276.
A.3
Notations and symbols
Table 4 summarizes the major notations and symbols used in the
main text of this paper.
Table 4: Notations and symbols.
Symb.
Explanation
DB
Database.
𝐷𝑖
𝑖-th DB dimension.
𝑛
LWE dimension (1,280 throughout this paper).
R𝑞
Polynomial ring Z𝑞[𝑋]/(𝑋𝑁+ 1).
𝑁
Degree of R𝑞(typically 212).
𝑞
Modulus of R𝑞.
𝑞𝑖
𝑖-th RNS prime composing 𝑞.
#RNS
The number of RNS primes composing 𝑞.
A.4
RNS prime selection
When selecting the RNS primes (𝑞𝑖’s), we make sure that
𝑞𝑖= 1 mod 2𝑁,
which is a sufficient condition for enabling NTT.
Further, we make sure that 2𝑁divides the RLWE plaintext mod-
ulus 𝑝and
𝑞𝑖= 1 mod 𝑝.
This RNS prime selection minimizes the errors from RLWE because
Δ · 𝑝+ 1 = 𝑞for scale Δ = ⌊𝑞
𝑝⌋.
With this setting, overflows/downflows in the plaintext domain
only result in small error increases. For example, 3
8𝑝+ 3
8𝑝→−1
4𝑝
in R𝑝translates to:
Δ · 3
8𝑝+ Δ · 3
8𝑝= Δ · 3
4𝑝= Δ ·

−1
4𝑝

−1
(mod 𝑞)
For the following sections, we ignore such errors in RLWE ci-
phertexts that stem from overflows/downflows in the plaintext
domain, as our RNS prime selection make them have minimal im-
pact on precision; errors from other sources (e.g., encryption) are
much greater. Then, we can calculate the phase of inter-ciphertext
addition and plaintext-ciphertext multiplication results of RLWE
ciphertexts as follows:
𝜙(ct + ct′) = Δ · (𝑚(𝑋) + 𝑚′(𝑋)) + (𝑒(𝑋) + 𝑒′(𝑋))
= 𝜙(ct) + 𝜙(ct′)
𝜙(𝑐(𝑋) · ct) = Δ · (𝑐(𝑋) · 𝑚(𝑋)) + (𝑐(𝑋) · 𝑒(𝑋))
= 𝑐(𝑋) · 𝜙(ct)
We can observe that the phase function 𝜙has a linear property
for both LWE and RLWE ciphertexts when ignoring the plaintext-
domain overflows/downflows.
Also, we select primes that are smaller than 229. This leaves
extra room for numbers beyond the typical ranges for numbers in
Z𝑞𝑖, specifically
h
−𝑞𝑖−1
2 , 𝑞𝑖−1
2
i
or [0,𝑞𝑖−1], which allows applying
modular reductions in a lazy manner [19, 33].
B
Experimental Materials Utilized
For the evaluation of prior studies, we mainly utilized their open-
source implementations listed in Table 5. We used the main branch
of each repository as of April 1st, 2026.
Table 5: Prior PIR studies and their data sources.
Prior studies
Data source (GitHub)
Spiral [46]
menonsamir/spiral
OnionPIRv2 [14, 49]
chenyue42/OnionPIRv2
HintlessPIR [39]
google/hintless_pir
Simple/DoublePIR [31] & YPIR [47]
menonsamir/ypir
InsPIRe [44]
google/private-membership
PIRonGPU [22]
Alisah-Ozcan/PIRonGPU
All implementations were evaluated using their default library
parameter configurations unless otherwise specified. We consider
DB containing 𝐷records in total. For OnionPIRv2, we used 𝐷0 = 29.
𝑁= 212, 𝑞≃2108, and plaintext modulus 𝑝= 232, which pro-
vides lower latency than the default configuration. For HintlessPIR,
SimplePIR, DoublePIR, and YPIR, we set 𝐷0 ≈
√
𝐷following their
default configurations. For InsPIRe, we selected 𝐷0 which mini-
mizes communication cost and also evaluated various 𝐷0 values
for Figure 1: 𝐷0 ∈{210, 211, 212} for 1GiB DB, 𝐷0 ∈{212, 213, 214}
for 4GiB DB, and 𝐷0 ∈{214, 215, 216} for 16GiB DB. Increasing 𝐷0 re-
duces execution time but increases communication cost, reflecting
13


--- Page 14 ---
Jongmin Kim, Hyesung Ji, Jean-Luc Watson, Charles Gouert, G. Edward Suh, and Jung Ho Ahn
the trade-off inherent in the protocol. PIRonGPU supports vari-
ous choices of 𝑁with significantly different performance; we used
𝑁= 215, which yields the lowest execution time.
C
Formal Description of ExpPack
We provide the formal specification of our expansion-based ring
packing (ExpPack) method, detailing the conversion from scalar-HE
(LWE) ciphertexts to poly-HE (RLWE) ciphertexts.
For simplicity, suppose we want to convert a collection of 𝑁LWE
ciphertexts to a single RLWE ciphertext, where each LWE ciphertext
encrypts a scalar value 𝑚𝑖(0 ≤𝑖< 𝑁) under the same secret vector
s = (𝑠0,𝑠1, · · · ,𝑠𝑛−1) ∈{−1, 0, 1}𝑛. These LWE ciphertexts can be
collectively represented as ctpack = (A, b), where A ∈Z𝑁×𝑛
𝑝
and
b ∈Z𝑁
𝑝. We are using 𝑝as the LWE ciphertext modulus and the
RLWE plaintext modulus at the same time.
This ctpack is constructed by setting the a-part of the 𝑖-th LWE
ciphertext as the 𝑖-th row of a, and the 𝑏-part as the 𝑖-th element
of b. Then, we can similarly define the phase of this collection of
LWE ciphertexts as:
𝜙(ctpack) = A · s + b =
(Δ · 𝑚0 + 𝑒0, Δ · 𝑚1 + 𝑒1, · · · , Δ · 𝑚𝑁−1 + 𝑒𝑁−1),
|𝑒𝑖| < Δ
2 .
C.1
Tiptoe-style ring packing
To facilitate packing, we perform a column-wise decomposition of
the LWE matrix A as A = [A0 | A1 | · · · | A𝑛−1]. This allows us to
rewrite the phase as a summation:
𝜙(ctpack) = b +
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
A𝑖· 𝑠𝑖= m
(mod 𝑝)
(2)
Tiptoe [30] leverages this linear structure for packing, utilizing
a method previously established in the context of column-based
ring packing [17]. Under this construction, the client prepares 𝑛
independent RLWE ciphertexts, each encrypting one element of
the secret vector s:
ctPack,𝑖←Encrypt(𝑠𝑖).
We define an isomorphism 𝜄that maps a length-𝑁vector over
Z𝑝to a polynomial in the ring R𝑝by treating the vector elements
as coefficients:
𝜄: Z𝑁
𝑝∋(𝑎0,𝑎1, . . . ,𝑎𝑁−1) ↦→
𝑁−1
∑︁
𝑗=0
𝑎𝑗𝑋𝑗∈R𝑝
By selecting RLWE parameters such that the plaintext domain is
R𝑝and the ciphertext domain is R𝑞(where𝑞is the RLWE ciphertext
modulus), the server can perform a homomorphic evaluation of
Eq. 2. The computation is formulated as:
(0, Δ · 𝜄(b)) +
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝜄(A𝑖) · ctPack,𝑖→ctres
(3)
In this expression, (0, Δ · 𝜄(b)) represents a trivial RLWE en-
cryption used to incorporate the offset vector b into the result; it
is trivial that 𝜙((0, Δ · 𝜄(b))) = Δ · 𝜄(b). The terms 𝜄(A𝑖) · ctPack,𝑖
denote plaintext-ciphertext multiplications. Because the phase com-
putation and 𝜄has a linear property, the phase of ctres becomes:
𝜙(ctres) = Δ · 𝜄(b) +
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝜄(A𝑖) · (Δ · 𝑠𝑖+ 𝑒𝑖(𝑋))
= Δ ·
 
𝜄(b) +
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝜄(Ai) · 𝑠𝑖
!
+
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝜄(A𝑖) · 𝑒𝑖(𝑋)
= Δ · 𝜄
 
b +
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
Ai · 𝑠𝑖
!
+
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝜄(A𝑖) · 𝑒𝑖(𝑋)
= Δ · 𝜄(m) +
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝜄(A𝑖) · 𝑒𝑖(𝑋)
Thus, ctres can be decrypted to obtain 𝜄(m) when the sum of the
error terms (𝜄(A𝑖) · 𝑒𝑖(𝑋)) is small enough.
To ensure computational efficiency, the server lifts the coeffi-
cients of 𝜄(A𝑖) and 𝜄(b) from Z𝑝to Z𝑞and transforms them into
the NTT domain. The resulting process enables the server to pack
multiple scalar LWE instances into a single RLWE ciphertext as
illustrated in Figure 2.
C.2
Expansion-based packing (ExpPack)
To minimize communication overhead, ExpPack replaces the re-
quirement for 𝑛individual ciphertexts with a single RLWE cipher-
text. The client constructs an RLWE ciphertext ctExpPack by encod-
ing the secret scalars 𝑠𝑖as coefficients of a single polynomial:
ctExpPack ←Encrypt
 𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝑠𝑖𝑋𝑖
!
(4)
Note that the specific assignment of 𝑠𝑖to coefficients may vary
depending on the chosen expansion schedule (e.g., bit-reversed
ordering).
To recover the individual encryptions, the server applies the
recursive expansion process described in SealPIR [4]. A known
property of this expansion method is that it yields ciphertexts en-
crypting 2⌈log2 𝑛⌉· 𝑠𝑖rather than 𝑠𝑖directly. To normalize these
values, the server pre-scales the initial ciphertext ctExpPack by the
modular inverse 2−⌈log2 𝑛⌉(mod 𝑞) prior to the expansion. While
2−⌈log2 𝑛⌉is a large integer in Z𝑞, it is canceled out by the 2⌈log2 𝑛⌉
scaling during expansion.
D
Security & Precision
The security of our protocols follows directly from the security of
their underlying components; we refer to SimplePIR [31], SealPIR [4],
and Tiptoe [30] for detailed analyses.
For precision, we require a more careful error analysis as HE
errors may propagate across PIR components.
D.1
Error isolation via reinterpretation and
ExpPack
When performing reinterpretation, encrypted data is treated as
a stream of plaintexts that are directly recovered by the client
via decryption. As a result, prior context about errors is lost, and
previously accumulated errors are not propagated to subsequent
steps. As a result, precision analysis can be conducted independently
14


--- Page 15 ---
VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols
for each segment separated by reinterpretation, most of which have
already been analyzed in SimplePIR [31].
Also, our ExpPack construction similarly treats scalar-HE ci-
phertexts as plaintexts (see Eq. 3), making packing errors largely
independent of previously accumulated errors.
Thus, the only remaining path that requires verification is Exp-
Pack followed by the poly-HE dimension folding steps in our hybrid
protocol.
D.2
Error analysis of scalar-HE rounding
Before performing the main computations of ExpPack, we reduce
the ciphertext modulus of scalar-HE ciphertexts from 232 to 𝑝=
218, following Tiptoe [30]. This is equivalent to introducing an
additional rounding error term into each element of a scalar-HE
ciphertext (𝑎0,𝑎1, · · · ,𝑎𝑛−1,𝑏) ∈Z𝑛+1
232 , which originally decrypts
as
𝑏· 𝑠𝑛+
𝑛−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝑎𝑖· 𝑠𝑖= Δ · 𝑚+ 𝑒.
We are using a ternary secret (𝑠0, . . . ,𝑠𝑛−1), and a scale Δ = 224.
We assume the previously accumulated error is sufficiently small:
|𝑒| < Δ
4 .
Let 𝑟a,𝑖(for 0 ≤𝑖< 𝑛) and 𝑟𝑏denote the rounding errors for
𝑎𝑖and 𝑏, respectively, where |𝑟𝑖| < 213. Each rounding error is in
range (−231
𝑝, 231
𝑝)
The total additional error becomes
𝐸round = 𝑟𝑏+
𝑛
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝑟a,𝑖· 𝑠𝑖.
To ensure correctness, it suffices to guarantee
|𝐸round| < Δ
4 .
Assuming that these rounding errors are i.i.d. uniform over
(−231
𝑝, 231
𝑝), where 𝑠𝑖values are also i.i.d. and is −1, 0, or 1, we
can bound the tail probability using Hoeffding’s inequality. Each
term lies in an interval of width 232
𝑝, and there are (𝑛+ 1) terms.
Thus,
Pr [|𝐸round| ≥𝑡] ≤2 exp

−
2𝑡2
(𝑛+ 1) · (232/𝑝)2

.
For this LWE rounding, we use parameters 𝑝= 218, which is also
used as the RLWE plaintext modulus, 𝑛= 1,280, and Δ = 224 (LWE
scale). When we plug in these values with 𝑡= 1
4Δ = 222, we obtain
Pr

|𝐸round| ≥Δ
4

≤2 exp(−102) < 2−146,
which is negligible. Even if we have trillion (240) ciphertexts, the
union bound probability (< 2−106) is still negligible.
D.3
Error analysis of ExpPack
For ExpPack, we show that the maximum (not probabilistic) error
remains sufficiently small. As mentioned before, the error from the
input scalar-HE ciphertexts have minimal impact as we interpret
them as plaintexts during packing. Suppose the error of ctPack,i
is bounded by |𝐸expand|. As we accumulate 𝑛plaintext-ciphertext
multiplication results as shown in Eq. 3, we can bound the total
error to 𝑛· |𝐸expand|. We make this sufficiently small by setting the
ciphertext modulus 𝑞large enough (e.g., 𝑞≃287).
|𝐸expand| is determined by the expansion process. If the error
of a freshly encrypted ciphertext is bounded by 6𝜎(typically 𝜎=
3.2), the maximum size of error from expansion is determined as
follows [46]:
|𝐸expand| < 2⌈log2 𝑛⌉· (ℓ𝑡+ 1) · 6𝜎.
Here, ℓis the decomposition degree for the expansion keys, and
𝑡is determined as
𝑡= 2⌈log2 𝑞
ℓ
⌉.
The parameters we use are 𝑛= 1,280, 𝑞≃287, ℓ= 5, 𝑡= 218, and
𝜎= 3.2. Plugging in these values, we roughly get |𝐸expand| < 235.6.
Thus, the maximum error from ExpPack with expansion is strictly
bounded by:
|𝐸ExpPack| < 𝑛|𝐸expand| < 246,
which does not affect the precision as it is much smaller than the
poly-HE ciphertext scale Δ = 𝑞
𝑝≃269 for 𝑝= 218.
D.4
Error in poly-HE folding for our hybrid
protocol
Although poly-HE folding steps directly follow ExpPack, the errors
introduced during poly-HE folding are additive and do not depend
on the errors already accumulated in the RLWE ciphertexts. As we
have shown that these prior errors remain sufficiently small relative
to the scale Δ, the remaining error analysis for poly-HE folding
follows directly from OnionPIR [49], which we defer a detailed
analysis to. The only difference is that the expansion process is
shared with that used for the special ExpPack ciphertexts.
D.5
Error analysis of RLWE modulus switching
At the end of each packing (except in our hybrid protocol, where it
is deferred to the end of the online server computation), we perform
modulus switching on the resulting RLWE ciphertexts, reducing the
modulus from 𝑞to approximately 229, corresponding to #RNS = 1.
This modulus reduction has similar effects to scalar-HE rounding.
For an RLWE ciphertext ct = (𝑎(𝑋) = 𝑎0 + 𝑎1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑎𝑁−1 ·
𝑋𝑁−1,𝑏(𝑋) = 𝑏0 + 𝑏1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑏𝑁−1 · 𝑋𝑁−1), which decrypts as:
𝑎(𝑋) · 𝑠(𝑋) +𝑏(𝑋) = (Δ ·𝑚0 + 𝑒0) + · · · + (Δ ·𝑚𝑁−1 + 𝑒𝑁−1)𝑋𝑁−1
for a ternary secret 𝑠(𝑋) = 𝑠0 + 𝑠1𝑋+ · · · + 𝑠𝑁−1𝑋𝑁−1 and scale Δ,
which is set to ⌊𝑞
𝑝⌋when coupled with scalar-HE rounding.
If we only look at the coefficient of 𝑋𝑘, the following holds:
𝑏𝑘+
𝑁−1
∑︁
𝑖=0
𝑎𝑖· 𝑠(𝑘−𝑖) mod 𝑁= Δ · 𝑚𝑘+ 𝑒𝑘.
Assume that the previously accumulated error is sufficiently small:
|𝑒𝑘| < Δ
4 .
When reducing the modulus from 𝑞to 229, each coefficient of
𝑎(𝑋) and 𝑏(𝑋) incurs a rounding error. Let 𝑟𝑎,𝑖denote the rounding
error for 𝑎𝑖and 𝑟𝑏,𝑖for 𝑏𝑖, where |𝑟𝑎,𝑖|, |𝑟𝑏,𝑖| <
𝑞
230 . The resulting
additional error for the 𝑘-th coefficient becomes
𝐸round = 𝑟𝑏,𝑖+
𝑁−1

𝑖=0
𝑟𝑎,𝑖· 𝑠(𝑘−𝑖) mod 𝑁.

--- Page 16 ---
Jongmin Kim, Hyesung Ji, Jean-Luc Watson, Charles Gouert, G. Edward Suh, and Jung Ho Ahn
To guarantee correct decryption, it suffices to ensure
|𝐸round| < Δ
4 .
Assuming the rounding errors are i.i.d. and uniformly distributed
in

−𝑞
230 , 𝑞
230

, we can bound the tail probability using Hoeffding’s
inequality. Since each term is bounded within an interval of width
𝑞
229 and there are (𝑁+ 1) terms, we have
Pr [|𝐸round| ≥𝑡] ≤2 exp

2𝑡2
(𝑁+ 1) · (𝑞/229)2

.
By plugging in our parameters, which are 𝑁= 212, 𝑝= 218,
Δ = ⌊𝑞

4 , we get:
Pr

≤2 exp(−128) < 2−183.
Even if we consider all the 𝑁= 212 coefficients and trillion (240)
ciphertexts, the union bound probability (< 2−131) remains negligi-
ble.
